# UC-23.3.4 · Website Traffic Source and SEO Performance

> **Criticality:** High &middot; **Difficulty:** Intermediate &middot; **Pillar:** Platform &middot; **Type:** Business, Analytics &middot; **Wave:** Crawl &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We count real visits from your web-server diary lines and sort them by whether people came from search sites, clicked tagged ads, followed social links, or typed the address with no referral. That tells you where audiences originate before you spend more on ads or fixing pages.*

---

## Description

Classifies human page views on Apache combined web logs into channel buckets using search-referrer domains, UTM query parameters on `uri`, and missing-referrer direct traffic — then rolls up approximate sessions (30-minute idle gaps on a hashed client fingerprint), visitors, engagement per channel, bounce rate from single-page sessions, and share of site sessions.

## Value

Growth and web teams steer crawl budgets, landing-page experiments, and paid versus organic mixes using comparable Splunk-native ratios rather than waiting on delayed CRM exports — seeing organic versus tagged-paid splits alongside bounce and pages-per-session highlights thin landing experiences before rankings slip in Search Console. Session-share shifts highlight concentration risk when one channel dominates acquisition; pairing Splunk-side classification with periodic exports from Search Console anchors interpretation when rankings stay flat but clicks move.

## Implementation

Install Splunk Add-on for Apache Web Server (3186), monitor access logs with referer and full URI preserved, assign `index=web` and `access_combined`, tune bot exclusions and static-asset filters, save the SPL as a weekly dashboard panel plus an optional alert when Organic session_share_pct drops sharply week-over-week.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites
- Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud search tier with rights to `index=web` (or rename indices consistently across SPL, roles, and inputs).
- Splunk Add-on for Apache Web Server (`Splunk_TA_apache`, Splunkbase 3186) on forwarders that tail logs and on Search Heads so field extractions align between ingestion and interactive search.
- Universal Forwarder or Heavy Forwarder with read access to Apache log files written by `mod_log_config`; disk capacity sized for combined-log volume plus seven to thirty days of local buffering.
- Confirm combined format includes quoted referer and user-agent fields — missing quotes from custom pipelines breaks `referer`/`useragent` extraction and silently corrupts channel logic.
- Coordinate with marketing analytics owners so Splunk channel labels (Organic versus Paid versus Direct) match internal glossary terms — this UC stays server-log-native and does not reconcile revenue or CRM touches (see UC-23.3.1 for finance-aligned ROI blends).

### Step 1 — Configure data collection
Point inputs at production VirtualHost logs using combined format — minimally `%h %l %u %t "%r" %>s %b "%{Referer}i" "%{User-agent}i"` — so each event exposes client IP, full request URI with query string, HTTP status, bytes, referer, and user-agent.

Deployment Server pattern: ship an app bundle (for example `web_apache_uf`) containing `Splunk_TA_apache` plus `local/inputs.conf`:

```ini
[monitor:///var/log/apache2/access.log]
disabled = 0
sourcetype = access_combined
index = web
```

Preserve query strings: avoid rewriting `%r` upstream of Splunk when UTMs must survive to `uri`. Rotate logs with copytruncate only when Splunk monitor semantics are validated — abrupt truncation can duplicate or orphan lines during busy campaigns.

Role hygiene: grant analysts `srchIndexesAllowed` that includes `web` without widening unrelated indexes.

Volume planning: busy sites exceed tens of gigabytes monthly — confirm license and retention policies before enabling noisy VirtualHosts.

Field naming drift: combined presets normally populate `referer` — corporate syslog relays occasionally lowercase headers — confirm search-time fields align (`referer` versus `referrer`) before adopting classifier SPL verbatim.

Docker or Kubernetes hosts: mount host log paths consistently into containers monitored by forwarders — symlink churn breaks tail checkpoints unless volumes remain stable across pod restarts.

### Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Clone the SPL from the top-level `spl` field into a saved search `web_channel_engagement_weekly`. Default time range last seven days aligned to `@d` boundaries so weekly comparisons stay repeatable.

Wrap volatile regex fragments inside macros (`macros.conf`) — bot suppression lists evolve quarterly — macros prevent sixty-plus deployments touching the same literal pattern.

Schedule options:
- Dashboard panels: fifteen-minute refresh for operations; disable acceleration unless you summarize via summary indexing.
- Digest alert (recommended starting posture): weekly Monday 07:00 local over `-7d@d`, trigger when Organic session_share_pct drops more than eight percentage points versus prior week AND sessions remain above a noise floor — route email to growth marketing and web engineering distribution lists rather than paging.

Alert SPL variant — compute prior-week Organic share with `append` — keep thresholds vendor-specific; document whichever gradient your leadership expects.

Before promoting alerts, clone to `sandbox_web` index during tuning so classifier tweaks do not spam stakeholders.

Ownership note: classify alerts under Web Analytics or Growth Operations — exclude CRM attribution debates explicitly — sibling UC-23.3.1 owns blended ROI scrutiny.

### Step 3 — Pipeline mechanics
Time bounds — opening clause restricts `_time` to seven rolling days ending now — tighten or widen only alongside dashboard narratives.

Status and noise trims — HTTP successes (`status` between two hundred and four ninety-nine) remove most probe failures while retaining redirects marketers sometimes track — drop redirect codes entirely if your classification must ignore short chains.

Static paths — favicon, robots, synthetic health probes excluded so engagement ratios reflect human navigations rather than monitors.

Bot suppression — regex on `useragent` drops common crawlers and scripted clients whose hits inflate impressions without conversions — tune quarterly because vendors rotate identifiers — intentionally overlaps Chrome automation strings sparingly — whitelist internal QA subnets via supplemental macros when scripted journeys must remain visible.

UTM extraction — `rex` on `uri` pulls `utm_medium` first — paid classification keys off lowercase medium tokens (`cpc`, `ppc`, `paidsearch`) — extend lists inside `paid`/`channel` blocks when finance adopts new naming.

Referer host — second `rex` anchors referer hostname — evaluated lowercase — dictionary membership promotes Organic search rows without trusting fragile full-query duplicates stripped by browsers.

Channel precedence — case statement prioritizes paid UTMs ahead of organic referer hits — reorder only if product insists organic wins when both signals appear.

Session approximation — md5 hash combines `clientip` plus `useragent` — idle gaps exceeding eighteen hundred seconds increment session counters — aligns loosely with analytics idle timeouts yet remains server-only — lacks JavaScript heartbeat fidelity — bounce underestimates sticky SPAs.

Per-session compaction — after counting events per session id, statistics derive visitors and bounce numerator — bounce equals sessions whose page count equals one — divide yields bounce rate.

Portfolio normalization — eventstats computes total sessions across channels — subsequent ratio expresses share — sorting exposes dominant acquisition vectors — totals omit bots owing to upstream filters — sudden inflation of Direct usually traces privacy stripping rather than indexer duplication.

### Step 4 — Validate
Spot-check ten URLs directly in the browser — confirm UTMs appear verbatim in Apache lines immediately after clicks — latency suggests buffering elsewhere.

Compare Splunk Organic sessions versus Search Console clicks on overlapping calendars — trends track loosely — gaps exceeding forty percent merit referer stripping investigations — compare clicks versus Splunk Organic rows inside spreadsheet pivot — annotate deltas referencing HTTPS downgrade tickets.

Pick twenty organic rows — verify referer host regex tagged them Organic — manually inspect remaining hosts landing in Referral bucket — widen regex cautiously — confirm Yahoo ccTLDs beyond `.com` remain anticipated edge cases.

Cross-read bounce_rate against analytics bounce definitions — large divergence implies SPA navigation absent from raw logs — instrument auxiliary beacon ingestion when parity matters legally.

Verify bot filter leaves intentional synthetic monitors intact — CI probes using curl should remain excluded — whitelist internal QA subnets via supplemental search macro if needed.

Confirm license usage stable post-deploy — unexpected duplication signals double-ingestion across rotated paths — scan `_indextime` skew distributions weekly — backlog spikes distort bounce counts temporarily.

Exercise classifier drift tests quarterly — replay archived `_raw` snippets through updated macros inside `makeresults` pipelines — prevents accidental Paid collapse during harmless punctuation tweaks.

### Step 5 — Operational caveats and known false positives
- Referrer-policy downgrade — HTTPS marketing pages linking HTTP landing pages strip referer entirely — Splunk logs show Direct despite organic intent — mitigation relies on HTTPS everywhere plus consistent tagging.

- Privacy-heavy browsers remove cross-site query strings — UTMs absent while campaigns active — Paid bucket empties without finance pause — reconcile via ad-platform IDs offline.

- GA4 thirty-minute idle aligns loosely — Splunk replication matches idle threshold chosen here yet SPA updates invisible to Apache skew engagement downwards versus GA.

- UTM persistence across redirects — marketing redirects strip parameters unless preserved server-side — Splunk classification misses Paid unless landing URI still carries markers.

- AMP caches fetch origin HTML indirectly — visitors arriving via AMP CDN often yield sparse referer capture — Organic looks understated unless supplemental beacon ingestion exists.

- Social in-app browsers rewrite hosts — Instagram or LinkedIn embedded browsers sometimes classify into Referral rather than Social until hostname lists expand.

- Algorithm surprises — impressions crater without Splunk movers — declare incidents only after correlating Search Console query deltas — avoid blaming infra when SERP layouts shift.

Governance firewall — differentiate explicitly from UC-23.3.1 CRM ROI narratives — Splunk queries here intentionally omit warehouse joins — finance pacing dashboards remain authoritative when reconciling acquisition dollars.


## SPL

```spl
index=web sourcetype="access_combined" earliest=-7d@d latest=now
| where status>=200 AND status<500
| where NOT match(uri,"(?i)(/favicon\\.ico|/robots\\.txt|/health)(?:\\?|$)")
| where NOT match(useragent,"(?i)(googlebot|bingbot|ahrefs|semrush|mj12|dotbot|petalbot|bytespider|gptbot|claudebot|anthropic|ccbot|perplexity|facebookexternalhit|whatsapp|python-requests|\\bcurl\\b|\\bwget\\b|spider|crawler)")
| rex field=uri "(?i)[?&]utm_medium=(?<utm_medium>[^&]+)"
| rex max_match=0 field=referer "(?i)^(?:https?:)?//(?<ref_host>[^/?:]+)"
| eval ref_host=lower(ref_host), utm_m=lower(trim(utm_medium))
| eval paid=if(match(utm_m,"(cpc|ppc|paidsearch|paid|display)"),1,0)
| eval channel=case(
    paid=1,"Paid — UTM",
    match(ref_host,"(?i)^(www\\.)?(google\\.[^/]+|bing\\.com|duckduckgo\\.com|yandex\\.(ru|com)|baidu\\.com|naver\\.com|yahoo\\.(com|co\\.uk)|search\\.brave\\.com|kagi\\.com)$"),"Organic — search",
    match(referer,"(?i)(facebook|instagram|linkedin|twitter\\.com|t\\.co|tiktok)"),"Social — ref",
    match(utm_m,"^social"),"Social — UTM",
    match(utm_m,"(email|newsletter|edm)"),"Email — UTM",
    isnull(referer) OR referer="-" OR match(referer,"^\\s*$"),"Direct",
    1=1,"Referral")
| eval sid=md5(clientip."|".coalesce(useragent,"-"))
| sort 0 sid _time
| streamstats window=1 global=f current=f last(_time) as prev_t by sid
| eval gap=if(isnull(prev_t),0,_time-prev_t)
| eval newseg=if(isnull(prev_t) OR gap>1800,1,0)
| streamstats global=f sum(newseg) as seg by sid
| eval session_id=sid."_".seg
| eventstats count as epv by session_id
| stats first(epv) as pages_in_session first(clientip) as cip by session_id channel
| stats dc(cip) as visitors sum(pages_in_session) as pageviews sum(eval(if(pages_in_session==1,1,0))) as bounce_sessions count as sessions by channel
| eval pages_per_session=round(pageviews/sessions,2), bounce_rate=round(100*bounce_sessions/sessions,1)
| eventstats sum(sessions) as site_sessions
| eval session_share_pct=round(100*sessions/site_sessions,1)
| sort - sessions
| table channel session_share_pct sessions visitors pageviews pages_per_session bounce_rate
```

## Visualization

Dashboard Studio or Classic: horizontal bar of `session_share_pct` by `channel`; sortable table of `sessions`, `visitors`, `pages_per_session`, `bounce_rate`; line chart of daily `sessions` split by `channel` from a scheduled report using the same SPL with `bin _time span=1d` inserted before the session rollups; single-value sparkline for week-over-week organic share delta.

## Known False Positives

A broad Google Search algorithm update can depress clicks and impressions in Search Console while your first-party Splunk session counts stay flat — the causal story is ranking and SERP volatility off-site, not a broken web-server feed or Splunk outage. Earned-media spikes (major press, broadcast, podcast) lift brand queries; organic Splunk rows rise with no matching on-site content change, and bounce can look high if readers land once and leave satisfied. Geo-IP database refreshes sometimes re-home mobile carrier ranges; analytics geography mixes swing while Apache sees identical subnets — resembling a regional acquisition shift without marketing intention. Country-domain redirect chains (`google.de` versus `google.com`) strip or reshape referers during tests — Direct rises briefly until hostname dictionaries absorb both ccTLDs and parent domains. Aggressive privacy modes null referers or strip query strings — Paid and Organic buckets shrink while Direct grows though invoices still reflect spend; reconcile against ad consoles rather than paging infra. Browser extensions and DNS-layer filters occasionally strip UTMs before navigation completes — Paid classification empties independent of bid strategy. Email or messaging opens using stripped preview frames yield clicks without meaningful referer — Splunk reads Direct though UTMs occasionally survive — blended tests confuse unless tagging lands on final HTTPS URLs. Mobile deep links jump straight into apps — landing URIs omit UTMs entirely unless SDK handlers append parameters server-side — Splunk understates attributed campaigns versus vendor dashboards. Single-page-app navigations after the first HTML fetch generate zero extra combined-log rows — engagement ratios diverge from analytics suites counting route transitions.
